title: 10 2 Half Range Expansions
body:
10.2 HALF RANGE EXPANSIONS


Suppose that a function f has period T = 2L. Then f may be approximated by the
Fourier series                      ∞
                                   X           nπx          nπx
                      f (x) = a0 +     (an cos     + bn sin     )
                                   n=1
                                                L            L
where the Fourier coefficients a0 , an , and bn are given by

                             1
                                  RL
                       a0 = 2L         f (x) dx
                                 −L


                                 RL               nπx
                       an = L1        f (x) cos       dx    (n = 1, 2, . . . )
                                 −L                L

                                 RL               nπx
                       bn = L1        f (x) sin       dx    (n = 1, 2, . . . )
                                 −L                L



In this lecture we will answer the critical question ‘If a function is not periodic how do
we find an applicable Fourier series?’ If the function is defined over the whole real line
then the answer is simple........you can’t! However if the function is only defined over an
interval (0, L) then it is quite simple to fool the Fourier series into believing that it is
looking at a periodic function by implementing what is called an odd or an even periodic
extension.


Example 1 Let f (x) = | x − 1 |            for 0 ≤ x < 3;


Sketch the periodic extension of f , the odd periodic extension of f and the even periodic
extension of f indicating the period of the extended function in each case.




                                                   1
 It is important to note in the above example that the original function f is definitely
non-periodic. We are however taking that small definition over (0, 3) and embedding it
within a periodic environment in a number of different ways. Observe in each of the three
graphs above you can still see the original f !


Example 2 Let f (x) = x2 + 1         for 0 ≤ x < 1;

Sketch the periodic extension of f , the odd periodic extension of f and the even periodic
extension of f indicating the period of the extended function in each case.




                                            2
 Now if a function is only defined over (0, L) we can easily extend it to a periodic function
using the trick above and then find its Fourier series! We are particularly interested in
the odd and even periodic extensions since the calculations for these functions are a little
less horrible and the pure sine and cosine series that are produced are easier to work with.

Note that if we take an even periodic extension then the Fourier series will be a Fourier
cosine series but if we take the odd periodic extension then we will end up with a Fourier
sine series. These are called Half Range expansions. Admittedly the sine and cosine series
will be defined over the whole real line rather than just (0, L), but that is OK as we can
choose to implement the series only over the restricted domain (0, L). In fact everything
beyond (0, L) is just a mirage! It is crucial to note that you DO NOT NEED special
formulae for half range expansions and the usual equations will serve you perfectly well,
provided you use a few simple tricks. Note also that we do not need to be too fussy about
what happens at endpoints and discontinuities since the Fourier series will always make
its own decision anyway and converge to a point which splits the gap.




                                             3
 Example 3 Let                            n x
                               f (x) =         + 1 0 ≤ x < 2;
                                           2
Find the Fourier sine series of f .


Note that we could simply extend f to a periodic function of period 2 in a trivial fashion
as follows:


                                                      2

                                                    1.5

                                                      1

                                                y   0.5

                                                      0
          -6            -4            -2                0        2             4                6
                                                    -0.5 t

                                                     -1




But this periodic function is neither odd nor even and hence the Fourier series will be
messy. We prefer the sine series.

The first step in calculating the Fourier sine series of f is to draw a careful sketch of the
odd periodic extension of f :
                                                      2


                                                      1


                                                      0
          -6            -4            -2                  0      2             4                6
                                                          t
                                                     -1


                                                     -2




                                                4
                                                           P∞ 2 − 4(−1)n )      nπx
                                           F fodd (x) =                   sin(     ) F
                                                          n=1    nπ             2


                                            6     πx  1         2     3πx
                            F fodd (x) =      sin( ) − sin(πx) + sin(     )...        F
                                            π      2  π         π      2


The graph of the sine series (taking the first 20 terms) is:

                                                 2

                                                 1 x
         -6            -4            -2               0         2            4                6
                                                 0

                                                 -1

                                                 -2



Note that we are really only interested in [0, 2] since that is where the original function
was defined! Note also that we have managed to approximate f using nothing but the
sine curve.

                                             5
 Example 4 Find the Fourier cosine series of the function f above.


First lets draw the even periodic extension:


                                                     2

                                                   1.5

                                                     1

                                           y       0.5

                                                     0
         -6           -4            -2                 0    2       4   6
                                                   -0.5 t

                     3 P∞ 2((−1)n − 1)      nπx     3 P∞      −4            (2k + 1)πx
F feven (x) =        +        2 2
                                      cos(     ) =  +            2  2
                                                                      cos(            ) F
                    2 n=1    nπ             2      2 k=0 (2k + 1) π             2


                                     3   4     πx   4      3πx      4        5πx
                     F feven (x) =     − 2 cos( ) − 2 cos(     )−     2
                                                                        cos(     )...   F
                                     2 π        2  9π       2     25π         2


   The graph of the cosine series (taking the first 6 terms) is:

               -6             -4           -2              0       2           4            6
